passages.explanation: Jesus says: truly I tell you, some standing here will not taste death before they see that the kingdom of God has come with power. After six days Jesus takes Peter, James, and John up a high mountain. There he's transfigured before them — his clothes become dazzling white, whiter than anyone in the world could bleach them. Elijah and Moses appear and are talking with Jesus. Peter says: Rabbi, it's good for us to be here. Let us put up three shelters — one for you, one for Moses, one for Elijah. He doesn't know what to say — they're so frightened. A cloud overshadows them, and a voice from the cloud: this is my Son, whom I love. Listen to him! Suddenly they look around and no longer see anyone with them except Jesus. Peter wants to build shelters — to preserve the moment, to keep Moses and Elijah alongside Jesus as equals. The voice corrects this: listen to him. Not to them. Jesus stands alone. Moses (the Law) and Elijah (the Prophets) have pointed to him and now they disappear.

As they come down the mountain, Jesus orders them not to tell anyone what they've seen until the Son of Man has risen from the dead. They keep the matter to themselves, discussing what 'rising from the dead' means. They ask: why do the teachers of the law say Elijah must come first? Jesus replies: Elijah does come first and restores all things. But why then is it written that the Son of Man must suffer much and be rejected? I tell you, Elijah has come, and they have done to him everything they wished, just as it is written about him. Elijah came as John the Baptist — and they killed him. The pattern is set: the forerunner suffers, and the one he announces will suffer too. The disciples can't process 'rising from the dead' because they can't process the dying that must precede it.

When they return to the other disciples, they find a large crowd and the teachers of the law arguing with them. As soon as the people see Jesus, they're overwhelmed with wonder and run to greet him. He asks: what are you arguing with them about? A man in the crowd answers: teacher, I brought you my son, who has a spirit that makes him unable to speak. Whenever it seizes him, it throws him to the ground. He foams at the mouth, gnashes his teeth, and becomes rigid. I asked your disciples to drive it out, but they couldn't. Jesus responds: you unbelieving generation, how long shall I stay with you? How long shall I put up with you? Bring the boy to me. When the spirit sees Jesus, it immediately throws the boy into a convulsion. The boy falls to the ground and rolls around, foaming. Jesus asks the father: how long has he been like this? From childhood, the father says — it has often thrown him into fire or water to kill him. But if you can do anything, take pity on us and help us. Jesus says: 'if you can'? Everything is possible for one who believes. Immediately the boy's father exclaims: I do believe — help me overcome my unbelief! Jesus rebukes the impure spirit: you deaf and mute spirit, I command you, come out of him and never enter him again. The spirit shrieks, convulses the boy violently, and comes out. The boy looks so much like a corpse that many say he's dead. But Jesus takes him by the hand, lifts him up, and he stands. When Jesus enters a house, the disciples ask privately: why couldn't we drive it out? He says: this kind can come out only by prayer. The father's confession — 'I believe, help my unbelief' — is one of the most honest prayers in Scripture. Jesus doesn't demand perfect faith; he works with honest faith.

They leave that place and pass through Galilee. Jesus doesn't want anyone to know where they are, because he's teaching his disciples: the Son of Man is going to be delivered into the hands of men. They will kill him, and after three days he will rise. But they don't understand what he means and are afraid to ask him. The second passion prediction. The disciples' fear of asking reveals they suspect they won't like the answer. They'd rather remain confused than confront what Jesus is saying.

They come to Capernaum. When he's in the house, Jesus asks: what were you arguing about on the road? They keep quiet, because on the way they'd argued about who is the greatest. Sitting down, he calls the Twelve: anyone who wants to be first must be the very last, and the servant of all. He takes a little child, has the child stand among them. Taking the child in his arms, he says: whoever welcomes one of these little children in my name welcomes me, and whoever welcomes me doesn't welcome me but the one who sent me. Jesus has just told them he's going to die. Their response is to argue about rank. He doesn't explode — he sits down (the posture of a teacher) and uses a child to illustrate the inversion. In that culture children had no status. Welcoming the statusless is the path to greatness.

John says: teacher, we saw someone driving out demons in your name and we told him to stop, because he wasn't one of us. Jesus replies: don't stop him. For no one who does a miracle in my name can in the next moment say anything bad about me. For whoever is not against us is for us. Truly I tell you, anyone who gives you a cup of water in my name because you belong to the Messiah will certainly not lose their reward. The disciples are gatekeeping. Jesus opens the gate. The kingdom is bigger than their group, and even small acts of kindness toward his followers count.

If anyone causes one of these little ones — those who believe in me — to stumble, it would be better for them to be thrown into the sea with a large millstone tied around their neck. If your hand causes you to stumble, cut it off. It's better to enter life maimed than with two hands to go into hell, where the fire never goes out. If your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off. It's better to enter life crippled than with two feet to be thrown into hell. If your eye causes you to stumble, pluck it out. It's better to enter the kingdom of God with one eye than with two eyes to be thrown into hell, where the worms that eat them don't die and the fire isn't quenched. Everyone will be salted with fire. Salt is good, but if it loses its saltiness, how can you make it salty again? Have salt among yourselves, and be at peace with each other. The language is hyperbolic but the point is deadly serious: whatever in your life leads you away from God, eliminate it ruthlessly. Partial discipleship that preserves the thing destroying you isn't worth the trade. Better to enter the kingdom damaged than miss it whole.
